title: Working with Factors in Analysis
description: Use factors in tables, plots, and modeling; convert between factors and characters.

slides.title: Factors in Tables 📊
slides.content:
The `table()` function creates frequency tables from factors. It respects the level order.

```r
f <- factor(c("yes", "no", "yes", "maybe"), levels = c("no", "maybe", "yes"))
table(f)
```

You can also create contingency tables from two factors: `table(f1, f2)`.

quizData.question: What does `table(factor(c("a","b","a")))` return?
quizData.explanation: table() returns a named vector with counts for each level.

slides.title: Factors in Plots
slides.content:
When you plot a factor, R automatically creates a bar plot. The order of bars follows the factor levels.

```r
plot(f)
```

This is why controlling level order is important for visualization.

slides.title: Simple Bar Plot
slides.codeSnippet:
f <- factor(c("red","blue","red","green"), levels = c("blue","green","red"))
plot(f)  # will show bars in blue, green, red order

slides.title: Converting Between Factors and Characters
slides.content:
- `as.character(f)` – converts factor to character vector (uses labels).
- `as.numeric(f)` – converts factor to its internal integer codes (rarely needed).
- `factor(ch)` – converts character to factor.

When reading data with `read.csv()`, character columns are often converted to factors by default. Use `stringsAsFactors = FALSE` to prevent this.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: If you need to change factor levels (e.g., combine categories), use `levels(f) <- newlevels`. But be careful: the mapping is by position, not value.
